title: Reward Management
simple_html:
Reward management is how an organisation decides what to pay people and how to recognise them for their work. It covers everything an employee gets in return for their effort — not just salary, but bonuses, pensions, benefits, praise, and opportunities to grow.

The core idea

People give an organisation their time, skills, and effort. In return, the organisation gives them rewards. Good reward management makes this exchange feel fair, attracts the right people, keeps them, and motivates the behaviour the business needs.

Two kinds of reward

  • Extrinsic rewards — tangible things you can see: base pay, bonuses, commission, pension, health insurance, company car. Often called the "total reward" package.
  • Intrinsic rewards — the feelings you get from the work itself: a sense of achievement, recognition, learning, and meaning. These cost little but matter enormously.

What makes reward "fair"?

Fairness has two sides. Internal equity means people doing similar-value jobs inside the company are paid similarly. External competitiveness means your pay is in line with what other employers offer for the same job. Get either wrong and people feel cheated or you lose them to rivals.

Key takeaways

  • Reward is the whole package, not just the salary number.
  • Extrinsic rewards attract; intrinsic rewards engage and retain.
  • Pay must feel fair internally and competitive externally.
  • Reward should be linked to the things the business values — if you reward it, you get more of it.
  • A reward strategy must fit the organisation’s wider strategy and culture.

In short, reward management is the deliberate design of "what we give for what we get" so that the organisation and its people both thrive.
intermediate_html:
Reward management is the formulation and implementation of strategies and policies that aim to reward people fairly, equitably, and consistently in line with their value to the organisation (Armstrong & Murlis). It sits at the intersection of motivation theory, labour economics, and strategy.

The total reward framework

Modern practice rejects the idea that pay alone drives behaviour. The total reward model groups everything an employee receives into four quadrants:

Transactional (tangible)Relational (intangible)
IndividualBase pay, bonuses, benefitsLearning & development, career
CommunalRecognition schemes, share plansWork environment, culture, voice

The transactional elements are easy to copy; the relational elements are where sustainable advantage lives because they are rooted in culture and harder for rivals to imitate.

Equity theory and the three equities

Adams’ equity theory holds that people compare their input/output ratio with that of relevant others. Perceived unfairness causes demotivation, conflict, or exit. Reward design therefore manages three equities: internal equity (consistent pay across comparable jobs, established through job evaluation), external equity (competitive with the labour market, established through pay benchmarking and surveys), and individual equity (differences within a grade based on performance or experience).

Building the structure

The typical mechanics are: (1) job evaluation — a systematic process (e.g., points-factor or job ranking) to rank roles by their relative size; (2) pay structure — grades and pay ranges, or broadbands, mapped onto those rankings; (3) market pricing — anchoring the structure to survey data; and (4) contingent pay — linking some pay to performance (individual bonus, team pay, profit-share, or skills/competency pay).

Worked example

A software firm wants to retain senior engineers. Benchmarking shows the market median base for the role is £85,000 but the firm pays £75,000 — an external equity gap driving attrition. The HR team:

  • Raises the grade midpoint to £85,000 (fixes external equity).
  • Sets a range of £76,500–£93,500 (±10%) so high performers can be paid above midpoint (individual equity).
  • Adds a 15% annual bonus tied to delivery and code-quality metrics (contingent pay aligned to strategy).
  • Strengthens relational reward: a clear technical career ladder and a conference budget (intrinsic motivation, low cost, high retention impact).

Attrition falls not just because base pay rose, but because the package now signals fairness and growth.

How to apply it

  1. Diagnose which equity is failing (run a job-evaluation and a market benchmark).
  2. Decide your pay positioning — lead, match, or lag the market (lead to attract scarce talent; lag if you compete on intrinsic rewards or stability).
  3. Align contingent pay with the specific behaviours strategy requires — beware rewarding A while hoping for B (Kerr’s classic warning).
  4. Communicate the total value; employees routinely undervalue benefits they cannot see.

For the MBA exam: be able to define total reward, the three equities, job evaluation vs. market pricing, and the risks of poorly designed contingent pay (gaming, crowding-out of intrinsic motivation).
full_html:
Reward management is the strategic and operational design of the financial and non-financial returns employees receive in exchange for their contribution. It is simultaneously a technical discipline (job evaluation, pay structures, benchmarking) and a behavioural one (motivation, fairness, signalling). This lesson develops both, grounds them in seminal scholarship, and links reward to the wider HR architecture covered in this course.

Definitions and philosophy

Armstrong & Murlis define reward management as being concerned with the strategies, policies, and processes required to ensure that the value of people and their contribution is recognised and rewarded. Three guiding principles recur: rewards should be fair, equitable, and consistent. The underlying exchange is captured by March & Simon’s (1958) inducements–contributions model: the organisation offers inducements sufficient to elicit the contributions it needs, and the employee stays while inducements exceed the perceived value of contributions.

The total reward concept

The dominant contemporary framework is total reward, popularised by the Towers Perrin / WorldatWork model and developed in the UK by Armstrong. It maps reward across two axes — transactional vs. relational, individual vs. communal — yielding four quadrants: pay; benefits; learning and development; and the work environment. The strategic argument (echoing the resource-based view, Barney, 1991) is that transactional rewards are easily imitated commodities, whereas relational rewards — culture, development, voice, recognition — are socially complex, causally ambiguous, and therefore a more durable source of advantage.

Motivation foundations

Reward design rests on motivation theory. Herzberg’s (1968) two-factor theory distinguishes hygiene factors (pay, conditions — their absence demotivates but their presence does not motivate) from motivators (achievement, recognition, growth). This implies pay alone has limits and that intrinsic reward drives sustained engagement. Vroom’s (1964) expectancy theory (effort → performance → reward → valued outcome) underpins all performance-related pay: contingent pay only motivates if employees believe effort will yield performance, that performance will be measured and paid, and that the reward is valued. Adams’ (1965) equity theory explains why fairness perceptions, not absolute amounts, govern satisfaction. Deci & Ryan’s (1985) self-determination theory adds a critical caveat: extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation, especially for inherently interesting work — a central debate explored below.

The three equities and how they are engineered

Internal equity is established through job evaluation — systematically assessing the relative size of jobs. Methods range from non-analytical (job ranking, paired comparison, job classification) to analytical (the points-factor method, e.g., the Hay Guide Chart Profile method, which scores know-how, problem-solving, and accountability). Analytical schemes are legally safer because they can defend equal-pay claims. External equity is established through market pricing — benchmarking roles against salary surveys and deciding a pay-positioning policy: to lead, match, or lag the market. Individual equity is handled by where a person sits within their pay range, driven by performance, competence, or tenure.

Pay structures

The outputs of evaluation and benchmarking are crystallised into a structure: narrow-graded structures (many grades, clear progression), broadbanding (few wide bands, flexibility but weaker control and grade-drift risk), job families (separate structures per function, e.g., a technical ladder alongside a managerial one), and pay spines (common in the public sector). Each balances control, flexibility, transparency, and cost.

Contingent and variable pay

Contingent pay links part of reward to results: individual performance-related pay (PRP), team-based pay, organisation-wide schemes (profit-sharing, gainsharing, employee share ownership), and skills- or competency-based pay. The evidence is mixed. PRP can sharpen line-of-sight and reward high performers, but Kohn (1993) and others argue it damages cooperation, encourages gaming, and crowds out intrinsic motivation. Kerr’s (1975) classic, On the folly of rewarding A while hoping for B, is the enduring warning: incentive systems reliably produce the behaviour they measure, not the behaviour intended. Designing measures that genuinely proxy strategic value is therefore the hard problem.

Worked example with calculation

A retail chain introduces a sales bonus: 5% of base for hitting a quarterly revenue target. An assistant on £24,000 base earns up to £300/quarter. Two problems emerge. First, gaming: staff discount-stack to hit revenue while eroding margin — the measure (revenue) diverged from the goal (profit). Redesign ties 60% of bonus to margin and 40% to a customer-satisfaction index, aligning measure to intent. Second, equity: high-traffic-store staff hit targets easily while quiet-store staff cannot, breaching procedural fairness. The fix indexes targets to store footfall. The lesson: contingent pay is only as good as its metric design and its perceived procedural justice (Greenberg, 1987).

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Treating pay as the whole of reward. It ignores the relational quadrants where retention is won.
  • Assuming more money always motivates. Herzberg and SDT show diminishing and sometimes negative returns.
  • Secrecy. Opaque pay breeds suspicion; transparency (within limits) supports perceived fairness.
  • Rewarding A while hoping for B. Poorly chosen metrics drive perverse behaviour.
  • Ignoring procedural justice. How reward decisions are made matters as much as the outcomes (Greenberg, 1987).
  • Failing to communicate total value. Employees systematically undervalue invisible benefits; total-reward statements correct this.

Cross-links within the syllabus

Reward is one leg of the AMO framework from Lesson 2 (Strategic HRM) — reward shapes the "Motivation" component of Ability–Motivation–Opportunity. It is tightly coupled to Performance Management (Lesson 4): contingent pay depends on credible appraisal, and the performance–reward linkage is where many systems fail. It connects to Employee Relations & Engagement (Lesson 7), since perceived pay fairness is a major driver of engagement and a frequent source of conflict and collective bargaining. Finally, in Talent, Wellbeing & HR Analytics (Lesson 8), reward analytics (pay-gap analysis, compa-ratios, attrition modelling) turn reward from art into evidence-based practice. A coherent reward strategy must align horizontally with these practices (bundling) and vertically with business strategy (best-fit) — the recurring strategic-HRM logic of this course.

Summary

Reward management designs the inducements–contributions exchange so it is fair (the three equities), motivating (expectancy and intrinsic–extrinsic balance), competitive (market positioning), and strategically aligned (total reward, bundled with other HR practices). Mastery means handling both the mechanics — job evaluation, structures, benchmarking, contingent-pay design — and the behavioural subtleties of fairness, signalling, and the ever-present risk of rewarding the wrong thing.
